text: Hello. This is a unedited, uncut, full length laparoscopic colisectomy video. And this is a simple gallbladder. We are using the four port colisectomy.

The surgeon uses a traumatic grasper to retract the gallbladder fundus towards the diaphragm. A semi-traumatic Hartsman pouch grasper holds the gallbladder while the harmonic device in the epigastric port performs adhesiolysis, slowly separating adhesions. The duodenum is visible during the procedure, and a stripping technique is applied with intramedial traction on the Hartsman pouch.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device to perform blunt dissection at the posterior peritoneum near the angle of the gallbladder body and cystic duct. The silicone jaw of the harmonic is positioned carefully against the wall to avoid puncturing, facilitating the creation of a posterior window.
The surgeon applies anterolateral traction on the gallbladder fundus, adjusting its position towards the right shoulder to facilitate anterior peritoneal dissection.

After removing the peritoneum and skeletonizing the cystic pedicle, the surgeon removes the harmonic device and introduces a Maryland dissector to further separate the peritoneum over the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector with its convexity oriented towards the liver to carefully create a posterior window between the cystic duct and cystic artery. Optimal traction is applied to avoid overextension, and the jaw of the Maryland is gradually opened to separate fibrous tissue, revealing the artery and duct beneath. Anterolateral and anteromedial traction is applied by the left hand to facilitate blunt dissection.

The surgeon defines Calot's triangle, identifying the liver, common hepatic duct, cystic artery, and cystic duct. The Maryland dissector is used to create an anterior window, and a clip is applied. An extracorporeal Misra's knot is tied around the cystic duct, with careful suture handling to prevent searing effects. The knot is secured with a sequence of hitches, winds, and locks, ensuring a secure closure without tenting the common bile duct.

The surgeon tightens the knot three times and uses scissors from the epigastric port to cut the suture tail, completing the ligation of the cystic artery. The cystic artery is securely ligated, and the harmonic device is used to seal the gallbladder, preventing leakage.

The surgeon applies a titanium clip to the cystic artery, ensuring the tip of the clip is visible. The harmonic device is used to complement the sealing effect of the clip, providing additional security against leakage.

The surgeon separates the gallbladder from the liver using anteromedial and anterolateral traction. The peritoneal attachments of the gallbladder are carefully dissected first, followed by the body of the gallbladder. The procedure is performed laparoscopically, with the gallbladder neck pushed towards the falciform ligament. Once half of the gallbladder is removed, the light cable of the 30-degree telescope is adjusted to improve visibility in the fossa. The fundus grasper is removed to prevent injury to the diaphragm, and traction is applied to complete the separation of the gallbladder from the liver.

The surgeon carefully inspects the gallbladder and liver bed to ensure no puncture or bleeding has occurred during the separation process. The light cable is adjusted for better visibility, allowing the surgeon to check the tightened stump and confirm the absence of bleeding or injury before completing the separation of the gallbladder from the liver.

The surgeon uses a 10 mm claw forceps to grasp the gallbladder at the level where the clip was applied. The neck of the gallbladder is then hidden into the cannula, which is subsequently removed from the surgical field.
